stmt: For every root state `p` and every finite set `R` with `q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, if `S₀ := {([# (p.2)], p.2)} ∩ R` and `S_{n+1}` is obtained from `Sₙ` by adding every `q ∈ R` having an `AtomicResidualGraphStep`-successor in `Sₙ`, then for every `n` and every `q ∈ R`, `q ∈ Sₙ` if and only if there is a path of length at most `n` from `q` to `([# (p.2)], p.2)`.
summary_delta: This sharpens the planned bounded-step saturation theorem into an exact distance criterion. It explains why finite saturation works, gives a reusable induction parameter, and isolates the real graph-theoretic content behind the eventual `|R|`-step corollary.
priority_rationale: This is the sharp distance theorem that would turn descent into exact convergence control.

stmt: For every root state `p` and every finite set `R` with `q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, the set `{q ∈ R | AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q}` is the least fixed point of the operator `F(S) := ({([# (p.2)], p.2)} ∩ R) ∪ {q ∈ R | ∃ r, AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ r ∈ S}` on subsets of `R`.
summary_delta: The solved result gives least closure; upgrading it to a genuine least fixed-point statement is the clean operator-level formulation needed for both iterative saturation and reverse-sweep correctness. It packages the recognizer in the presentation-independent form the current summary still lacks.
priority_rationale: Useful operator-level packaging of the exact accepting set, but mostly a reformulation of existing least-subset results.

stmt: Let p be an AtomicResidualState, let R be the finset of states reachable from p, and let L be a nodup reverse-topological list enumerating exactly R. For any suffix S of L, run the backward update on S from right to left, marking a state true exactly when it is the base state ([# (p.2)], p.2) or it has an AtomicResidualGraphStep to a state already marked true; states outside S are marked false. Then for every q in S, the resulting Boolean mark on q is true if and only if q reaches the base state by a path whose vertices all lie in S.
summary_delta: formalizer returned stuck before Lean verification
priority_rationale: Strong suffix-path semantics for arbitrary reverse-topological sweeps, but narrower than the full fold theorem.

stmt: A path decomposition lemma for reverse-topological lists: if q reaches the base state inside a processed suffix, then either q is the base state or q has a successor later in the suffix that also reaches the base state.
priority_rationale: This path decomposition inside a suffix is a sharp local bridge that could materially derisk the main invariant.

stmt: For every root state `p` and every finite set `R` with `q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q`, if `S0` is the singleton set `{([# (p.2)], p.2)}` intersected with `R` and `S_{n+1}` is obtained from `S_n` by adding every `q ∈ R` having an `AtomicResidualGraphStep`-successor in `S_n`, then `S_{|R|} = {q ∈ R | AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q}`.
summary_delta: This gives an order-free finite algorithm, not just an abstract closure principle. Because the reachable region is finite and degree-descending, a bounded-step saturation theorem would unify the structural and executable sides of the theory without reintroducing special list constructions.
priority_rationale: An executable saturation bound matters, but it is largely a corollary once exact distance control is proved.

stmt: For every contexts Γ, Δ, Λ and formulas A and B, if SupportClosure Γ A and SupportClosure (Δ ++ [A] ++ Λ) B, then SupportClosure (Δ ++ Γ ++ Λ) B.
summary_delta: This would turn the new SupportClosure = support_ok theorem into a cut-style composition principle, making support closure a reusable structural calculus rather than a one-off predicate.

stmt: For every nonempty context Γ and formulas A and B, SupportClosure Γ (A ⧹ B) if and only if SupportClosure ([A] ++ Γ) B.
summary_delta: This is the exact left-residuation law for ordinary support closure, upgrading the new theorem into a structural rule that matches the Lambek introduction-invertibility pattern.

stmt: For every nonempty context Γ and formulas A and B, SupportClosure Γ (B ⧸ A) if and only if SupportClosure (Γ ++ [A]) B.
summary_delta: This is the right-residuation counterpart, and together with the left law it would show that ordinary support closure respects the full directional structure of the calculus.

stmt: There exist a context Γ and a formula A such that SupportClosure Γ A holds but Γ ⇒ A does not.
summary_delta: A verified separation would identify the exact role of the new theorem by showing that support closure is a sharp necessary condition but not yet a complete derivability criterion.

stmt: If two contexts Γ and Δ contain exactly the same atomic names and are either both empty or both nonempty, and two formulas A and B contain exactly the same atomic names, then SupportClosure Γ A if and only if SupportClosure Δ B.
summary_delta: This would classify ordinary support closure as a genuine support invariant, explaining precisely what information the closure relation forgets and why the new equivalence is structurally robust.

stmt: For every context Γ and formula A, ordinary SupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if support_ok Γ A holds.

stmt: A clean clause-by-clause comparison between the SupportClosure rules and the recursive definition of support_ok
Local inversion lemmas matching each support constructor with the corresponding support_ok witness
For every context Γ and formula A, if every formula in Γ is left-only and A is left-only, then SupportClosure Γ A implies leftTranslatedHandedSupportClosure Γ A.
summary_delta: This turns the verified negative boundary into a sharp one-way bridge from ordinary support to the translated exact left-handed notion, clarifying how much of the full support approximation survives on same-handed fragments.

stmt: For every context Γ and formula A, if every formula in Γ is right-only and A is right-only, then SupportClosure Γ A implies rightTranslatedHandedSupportClosure Γ A.
summary_delta: This is the right-handed counterpart of the same bridge theorem and would package the surviving ordinary-support information in the only direction not ruled out by the verified counter-boundary.

stmt: There exist contexts Γ and formulas A and B such that every formula in Γ is left-only, A is left-only, B is right-only, HandedSupportClosure Γ A, HandedSupportClosure Γ B, and SupportClosure Γ A and SupportClosure Γ B both hold.
summary_delta: A mixed-goal separation would show that ordinary support forgets handed orientation even when the context is purely one-sided, isolating the exact obstruction behind the non-collapse theorem.

stmt: For every context Γ and formula A, if every formula in Γ is left-only and A is left-only, then HandedSupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if there exists a derivation of A from Γ in the left fragment after translating Γ and A to the left-only calculus.
summary_delta: The proved translation invariance makes an exact correspondence with the intrinsic left fragment newly plausible and would convert the theorem into a reusable criterion tied to one-sided proof theory and decidability.

stmt: For every context Γ and formula A, if every formula in Γ is right-only and A is right-only, then HandedSupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if there exists a derivation of A from Γ in the right fragment after translating Γ and A to the right-only calculus.
summary_delta: This is the symmetric right-fragment exactness theorem; together with the left version it would reclassify handed support closure as an intrinsic one-sided proof-theoretic notion rather than an ambient predicate.
